skill.name: postgresql
skill.description: PostgreSQL reference guidance for SQL, schema design, administration, installation, connection handling, and performance tuning.
skill.summary: Use PostgreSQL documentation guidance for SQL, database design, administration, and tuning tasks.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, crypto, and obfuscation patterns, but review found them embedded in PostgreSQL documentation and examples rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, automatic command execution, or malicious intent was found. Publish with low risk because the skill discusses administrative commands and database connection material that users should review before applying.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains Administrative Shell Commands
low_findings.description: TRUE SOURCE, FALSE POSITIVE. The scanner flagged shell commands and user switching examples, including source installation steps. These are PostgreSQL installation instructions in Markdown examples, not commands run by the skill. The content can still guide users toward privileged operations, so publication should note that users must review commands before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are fenced documentation examples for installing PostgreSQL and initializing a database. I found no surrounding mechanism that automatically executes these commands.

low_findings.title: Network and Filesystem Strings Are Reference Material
low_findings.description: TRUE SOURCE, FALSE POSITIVE. URLs, FTP references, local paths, hidden shell profile files, and device paths appear as PostgreSQL documentation examples. They do not form a download routine, file access routine, or exfiltration channel inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are documentation URLs and platform setup notes. There is no executable script, installer, or code path that reads local files or contacts these URLs.

low_findings.title: Long Lines and Security Keywords Come From Generated Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Extremely long lines in SKILL.md contain concatenated PostgreSQL reference text about connection strings, SSL, OAuth, passwords, roles, and server files. The keywords are relevant to database security documentation, not ransomware, keylogging, or obfuscated malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The long lines are readable PostgreSQL documentation extracts. They are poor formatting and high-noise for scanners, but they do not contain encoded payloads or evaluator override text.

content.value_statement: PostgreSQL work often requires exact syntax and careful administration choices. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured reference help for SQL, schema design, setup, and tuning.
actual_capabilities: Explains PostgreSQL SQL syntax, aggregate functions, inheritance, and query behavior.
Guides schema design discussions with PostgreSQL table, view, role, and object concepts.
Summarizes installation and setup documentation for source builds and binary installs.
Supports administration questions about roles, privileges, configuration, and server operation.
Provides reference context for connection strings, libpq behavior, SSL, OAuth, and authentication.
Helps reason about performance-related settings, indexes, query plans, and maintenance commands.
limitations: It does not connect to a live PostgreSQL database or inspect production state.
It cannot verify environment-specific commands before a user runs them.
Some generated documentation lines are very long and may be harder to scan manually.
It should not replace official release notes for version-specific operational changes.

use_cases.title: Write safer SQL queries
use_cases.description: Ask for PostgreSQL syntax guidance, query rewrites, aggregate behavior, or transaction semantics before changing application code.

use_cases.title: Plan database administration tasks
use_cases.description: Review installation, roles, privileges, configuration, and maintenance concepts before applying changes to a PostgreSQL server.

use_cases.title: Design reliable data models
use_cases.description: Use PostgreSQL reference context to compare tables, views, constraints, inheritance, large objects, and schema organization.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a PostgreSQL concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this PostgreSQL concept in simple terms, then show when I should use it: [concept].

prompt_templates.title: Review a query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this PostgreSQL query for correctness, performance, and safer alternatives: [query].

prompt_templates.title: Plan an admin change
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a careful PostgreSQL administration plan for [task]. Include prerequisites, risks, rollback steps, and commands to verify manually.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze production behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this PostgreSQL performance or reliability issue using the provided schema, query plans, logs, and constraints: [details].

output_examples.input: Explain why my aggregate query cannot use max in the WHERE clause.
output_examples.output: A clear explanation of PostgreSQL query evaluation order, why WHERE runs before aggregation, and how to rewrite the query with a subquery or HAVING.

output_examples.input: Review my plan to install PostgreSQL from source on a Linux server.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering prerequisites, build steps, user and directory setup, service startup checks, and warnings about privileged commands.

output_examples.input: Help design roles for a read-only reporting user.
output_examples.output: A role design summary that separates login access, schema permissions, table grants, and review points for row-level security.

best_practices: Share PostgreSQL version, schema details, and exact error text when asking for help.
Review generated SQL and shell commands in a safe environment before production use.
Use least-privilege roles and explicit permissions for administration and application access.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production passwords, private keys, or full connection strings into prompts.
Do not run privileged installation or maintenance commands without understanding their effects.
Do not assume examples match your PostgreSQL version or operating system without checking.

faq.question: Can this skill connect to my database?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference guidance only. It does not open network connections or inspect a live database.

faq.question: Does it include PostgreSQL administration guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes documentation about installation, configuration, roles, privileges, and maintenance topics.

faq.question: Can it write SQL for Claude, Codex, or Claude Code workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help draft and review PostgreSQL SQL, but users must validate results with tests and database tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: The audit found low risk. Static alerts were documentation examples, not executable malicious behavior.

faq.question: Why were security alerts reported?
faq.answer: The documentation contains shell commands, URLs, file paths, and security terms that scanners commonly flag.

faq.question: What should users avoid sharing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid sharing secrets, production credentials, private keys, and sensitive customer data in prompts.
